summary: mathepy silently relays caller-supplied prompts to author's Groq account via hardcoded API keys
details: Package is advertised as 'Module for Quick Calculations' but its public API surface (ask_llm, pink, america, iran, momo, dropnull, code, sf, abc, liti, bcd, hellp, init, lc, koko) constructs a Groq client with hardcoded `gsk_*` API keys belonging to the package author and forwards caller-supplied prompt strings to api.groq.com. For example, src/mathepy/ai_helper.py:4 instantiates `Groq(api_key="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")` and src/mathepy/pink.py:4 / src/mathepy/init.py:22 use the same pattern with ~14 different author-owned keys across modules. A caller invoking what looks like a math/calculation helper has their input data routed through a third-party LLM provider under the author's account, with no disclosure. The package metadata is placeholder-shaped (author `fakehu <fakehu45@gmail.com>`, generic description, README referencing an unrelated 'sample-package' name) and iran.py has a bare undefined identifier `ligt` that breaks import — corroborating low-quality / deceptive packaging. Independently, every call from the package leaks the prompt content to api.groq.com using the author's credentials, satisfying the silent-relay attack class: normal use of the advertised API silently leaks caller-supplied data to a destination the author controls.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
